startScreenOptions.title: PY10.1-PY10.9 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 5 embedded questions for PY10.1-PY10.9.

interactions.label: In Brown-Séquard syndrome (hemisection of the spinal cord), which combination of

params.question: In Brown-Séquard syndrome (hemisection of the spinal cord), which combination of deficits is expected below the level of the lesion?

answers.text: Ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature; contralateral loss of fine touch

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is reversed. The spinothalamic tract (pain, temperature) crosses within the cord, so its loss is contralateral. The DCML pathway (fine touch) crosses at the medulla, so its loss is ipsilateral.

answers.text: Bilateral loss of pain and temperature with preserved fine touch

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Brown-Séquard involves a unilateral hemisection, producing asymmetric deficits — not bilateral loss. Bilateral pain and temperature loss suggests a central cord lesion such as syringomyelia.

answers.text: Ipsilateral loss of fine touch and proprioception; contralateral loss of pain and temperature

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The DCML pathway ascends ipsilaterally before crossing at the medulla, so fine touch and proprioception are lost on the same side. The spinothalamic tract crosses within the cord, so pain and temperature are lost on the opposite side.
Because the two main sensory tracts cross at different levels (cord vs medulla), hemisection produces dissociated loss — different modalities lost on different sides, not all modalities on one side.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the two ascending pathways: the DCML crosses at the medulla (ipsilateral fine touch loss), and the spinothalamic crosses within the cord (contralateral pain and temperature loss).
The difference in crossing levels — spinothalamic in the cord, DCML at the medulla — is the anatomical basis for Brown-Séquard's characteristic dissociated sensory loss.

interactions.label: Which combination of signs correctly distinguishes an upper motor neuron (UMN) l

params.question: Which combination of signs correctly distinguishes an upper motor neuron (UMN) lesion from a lower motor neuron (LMN) lesion?

answers.text: UMN: flaccid paralysis with muscle wasting; LMN: spastic paralysis with hyperreflexia

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is reversed. Flaccidity and wasting indicate loss of the lower motor neuron's direct innervation. Spasticity results from loss of cortical inhibition on an intact lower motor neuron.

answers.text: UMN: spastic paralysis, hyperreflexia, positive Babinski sign; LMN: flaccid paralysis, areflexia, muscle wasting, fasciculations

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. In UMN lesions the muscle remains innervated (no wasting), but cortical control is lost — producing spasticity and exaggerated reflexes. In LMN lesions the muscle loses its nerve supply, causing flaccidity, wasting, and fasciculations.

answers.text: UMN: fasciculations and absent reflexes; LMN: clonus and increased tone

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is reversed. Fasciculations and areflexia are LMN signs. Clonus and increased tone are UMN signs resulting from loss of descending cortical inhibition.

answers.text: Both produce identical motor deficits distinguishable only by imaging

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: UMN and LMN lesions produce clinically distinct patterns reliably distinguished by bedside examination — tone, reflexes, wasting, and plantar response all differ systematically.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: UMN = spastic, hyperreflexic, Babinski positive, no wasting. LMN = flaccid, areflexic, wasting, fasciculations. Whether the muscle retains its direct innervation determines the pattern.
The key principle: in UMN lesions the lower motor neuron is intact (no wasting) but unsupervised (spastic, hyperreflexic). In LMN lesions the final common pathway is destroyed.

interactions.label: A patient suffers a stroke affecting the ventral posterolateral (VPL) nucleus of

params.question: A patient suffers a stroke affecting the ventral posterolateral (VPL) nucleus of the thalamus. Which clinical presentation is most likely?

answers.text: Loss of vision in the contralateral visual field

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Visual relay occurs through the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN), not the VPL. A VPL lesion affects body sensation, not vision.
Auditory relay occurs through the medial geniculate nucleus (MGN), not the VPL. Moreover, cortical hearing is bilaterally represented, so unilateral thalamic lesions rarely cause deafness.

answers.text: Loss of smell on the ipsilateral side

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Smell is the one sensory modality that bypasses the thalamus entirely, so thalamic lesions of any nucleus do not affect olfaction.

answers.text: Contralateral sensory loss followed by spontaneous burning pain

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A VPL stroke causes Dejerine-Roussy syndrome — initial contralateral sensory loss, followed by emergence of spontaneous thalamic pain that is burning, poorly localised, and resistant to treatment.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: VPL = body sensation. LGN = vision. MGN = hearing. Smell bypasses the thalamus entirely. VPL damage causes Dejerine-Roussy syndrome — sensory loss followed by thalamic pain.

The VPL relays body sensation to the somatosensory cortex. Its destruction initially abolishes contralateral sensation, then paradoxically produces intractable thalamic pain — Dejerine-Roussy syndrome.

metadata.title: A patient suffers a stroke affecting the

interactions.label: During REM sleep, the EEG shows slow delta waves similar to deep (stage N3) slee

params.question: During REM sleep, the EEG shows slow delta waves similar to deep (stage N3) sleep.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This is actually false. During REM sleep the brain is highly active and the EEG resembles wakefulness — not deep sleep. Delta waves define stage N3. REM shows fast, desynchronised activity despite the body being paralysed, which is why it is called paradoxical sleep.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this is false. REM sleep EEG paradoxically resembles wakefulness with fast, desynchronised activity, even though the body is paralysed. Delta waves are characteristic of stage N3 (slow-wave sleep), not REM.

metadata.title: During REM sleep, the EEG shows slow del

interactions.label: Which type of memory depends on the hippocampus and involves conscious recall of

params.question: Which type of memory depends on the hippocampus and involves conscious recall of facts and events?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Declarative (explicit) memory — covering facts and events — is conscious and depends on the hippocampus and medial temporal lobe. Bilateral hippocampal damage causes anterograde amnesia, the inability to form new memories.
Non-declarative (implicit) memory covers skills, habits, and conditioning. It is unconscious and depends on the basal ganglia, cerebellum, and amygdala — not the hippocampus.
Procedural memory (motor skills and habits) is a subcategory of non-declarative memory. It relies on the basal ganglia and cerebellum, not the hippocampus.
Sensory memory is the millisecond-duration buffer for incoming stimuli. It precedes short-term memory and does not require the hippocampus for its brief storage.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: declarative = conscious recall of facts and events, depends on the hippocampus. Non-declarative = unconscious skills and habits, depends on the basal ganglia, cerebellum, and amygdala.
Declarative memory (conscious, hippocampus-dependent) versus non-declarative memory (unconscious, basal ganglia and cerebellum) is a fundamental classification in memory physiology.
